metadata.title: BMF — Backtracking Model Form (2000)
metadata.description: The parser layer of the 2000 Bjorg-Muff thesis. BNF augmented with execution elements: when a rule matches, code fires. The grammar is itself executable BML — the system describes its own grammar in its own language.

hero.overlay_class: absolute inset-0 bg-gradient-to-t from-background via-background/85 to-background/30
hero.name: BMF — Backtracking Model Form
hero.welcome_md: BNF augmented with execution. When a rule matches, code fires. Expressions are tagged and placed on a structured stack each rule transforms into the target language's object model. The grammar file `BMF-grammar.bml` is itself written in [BML](/people/bml-language) — banner reads `Digi4Fun (R) BMF 1.0 Alpha 1`.

facts.label: Implementation
facts.value_md: C++ top-down recursive-descent parser with backtracking stack

facts.label: Grammar
facts.value_md: Self-describing — the grammar of BMF is written in [BML](/people/bml-language) (see `companion/source-samples/BMF-grammar.bml` in the archive)

facts.label: Trio
facts.value_md: BMF (parser) · BMA ( [assembler / BMCPU](/people/bmcpu-vm) ) · BMO (object model) — the three-tech split named in `bml-search-algorithms.txt`

facts.label: Ancestry
facts.value_md: [BNF](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Backus%E2%80%93Naur_form) (Backus 1959 · Naur 1960) · executable grammar concept anticipated yacc/bison

note_from_body.body_md: Parser is the first noun. *Executable grammar* is the second. BMF rules don't *describe* a parse tree — they *build* one as they go, with code firing on every successful match and a stack standing ready to undo every side effect on failure. The grammar is alive at parse time.

articles.body_md: The applied paper `bml-search-algorithms.txt` names the three-tech split that holds the system together. Each technology owns one altitude:
articles.heading: BMF / BMA / BMO — the three-tech split
The grammar declares itself in BML
What BMF rules actually look like

articles.body_md:
BMF rules are not pure recognizers. Each rule's right-hand side carries action code that fires on a match. Expressions on the parse stack are tagged so an action can lift the value of any subexpression by name. When a rule fails mid-way through — say, the third alternative of a `choose` block doesn't match — the runtime walks back through every tagged stack entry and **undoes the side effects each action introduced**, then tries the next alternative.

The same shape recurs three times in the system. At the parser level it's grammar backtracking. At the BMA ( [BMCPU](/people/bmcpu-vm) ) level it's the `DO`/ `UNDO` mode flag — every assembly instruction has a forward and a reverse semantics. At the language level ( [BML](/people/bml-language) ) it's `choose` / `Cut` / `Fail` / `MultiMatch`. The unwinding pattern threads through every layer.
articles.heading: Choose, backtrack, undo

articles.body_md: The acronym is a deliberate pun. **BMF** reads publicly as Backtracking Model Form; reads privately, in the team, as *Bjorg-Muff Form*, with the same cadence as BNF (Backus-Naur Form). Backus and Naur described grammars that machines could parse; Bjorg and Muff described grammars that machines could parse *and* execute *and* undo. Same prefix, one octave up.
articles.heading: Bjorg-Muff Form

en.footer_md: Source archive: [master-thesis-2000/companion/source-samples/](https://github.com/seeker71/Coherence-Network/tree/main/docs/field/urs/artifacts/master-thesis-2000/companion/source-samples) — BMF-grammar.bml, BMF-includes.bml, container-Rule.bml, primitive-Cut.bml. The published thesis lives one folder up at [backtracking-model-languages.txt](https://github.com/seeker71/Coherence-Network/blob/main/docs/field/urs/artifacts/master-thesis-2000/backtracking-model-languages.txt).
